perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and all matters arising out of or relating to this Agreement shall be governed by the procedural and substantive laws of the state of California and shall be deemed executed in Redwood City, California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and all matters arising out of or relating to this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of a jurisdiction chosen at Oracle's sole discretion, regardless of conflict of laws principles, and shall be deemed executed in Redwood City, California.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies California law. The modified clause allows Oracle to choose any jurisdiction, potentially undermining the initial agreement on California law, especially regarding mandatory disclosures.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts California Civil Code Section 1646, which dictates that a contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed. By allowing Oracle to unilaterally select the governing law, it undermines the principle that California law should apply given the contract's execution in Redwood City.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1646 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1646. A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts California Civil Code Section 1646. The original text specifies California law governs the agreement, while the changed text allows Oracle to choose any jurisdiction's law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'A contract is to be interpreted according to the law and usage of the place where it is to be performed; or, if it does not indicate a place of performance, according to the law and usage of the place where it is made.' Since the contract is deemed executed in Redwood City, California, California law should apply. The modification allows Oracle to circumvent this, creating a legal contradiction by deviating from the legally recognized principle that the law of the place of performance or execution should govern.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that money damages will not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by it or its Representatives, and that the other party is entitled specific performance and injunctive relief as remedies for any such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that specific performance and injunctive relief may be considered but are not guaranteed remedies for any breach of this Agreement by it or its Representatives, and that the availability of such remedies is at the court's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides for specific performance and injunctive relief. The modified text makes these remedies discretionary, potentially conflicting with legal principles regarding contract enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3384 states that a party is entitled to specific performance under certain conditions. By making specific performance discretionary, the modification potentially contradicts this law, making enforcement uncertain.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3384 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Specific Relief [3375 - 3415] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 3. Specific Performance of Obligations [[3384.] - 3395] ( Article 3 enacted 1872. ) [3384.] Section Thirty-three Hundred and Eighty-four. Except as otherwise provided in this Article, the specific performance of an obligation may be compelled. (Amended by Code Amendments 1873-74, Ch. 612.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific performance and injunctive relief, but the changed text makes these remedies discretionary. California Civil Code Section 3384, as presented in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that "the specific performance of an obligation may be compelled." The modified text directly contradicts this by making specific performance not guaranteed and subject to the court's discretion, thus weakening the legal right to compel specific performance, which creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: “Evaluation Material” also includes notes, analyses, compilations, studies, plans, interpretations or other documents prepared by the receiving party or its Representatives which contain or are based upon the Evaluation Material furnished to such party hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: “Evaluation Material” only includes the exact documents initially disclosed, and not any derivative works, analyses, or interpretations created by the receiving party or its Representatives, unless explicitly labeled as 'Evaluation Material'.
perturbation.explanation: By narrowing the definition, the revised text allows the receiving party to freely use insights gained from the disclosed material, potentially undermining the confidentiality intended.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification potentially contradicts the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), Cal. Civ. Code § 3426, which protects trade secrets from misappropriation. While the initial definition aligns with protecting derivative works based on trade secrets, the altered definition narrows the scope of protection, potentially allowing for the misappropriation of insights and analyses derived from the original Evaluation Material, thus violating the intended protection of the trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of "Evaluation Material," potentially allowing the receiving party to misappropriate insights and analyses derived from the original material. This contradicts the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), as the altered definition weakens the protection afforded to derivative works based on trade secrets. Snippet 1 cites Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 which establishes the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The modification undermines the protection of trade secrets, leading to a direct legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon notice from either party that it does not wish to proceed with the Transaction, each party shall return to the other party or destroy all copies of the Evaluation Material (including all documents based thereon) in its possession or in the possession of its Representatives, except that outside counsel to the receiving party may retain one copy of the Evaluation Material for archival purposes and solely to comply with applicable law, rule or regulation.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of discussions, each party may retain Evaluation Material indefinitely for internal research and development purposes, provided that the material is not directly disclosed to third parties. There is no obligation to return or destroy the Evaluation Material.
perturbation.explanation: This change negates the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, conflicting with standard practices for protecting trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly about non-compete agreements, this relates to the protection of trade secrets. The modification contradicts the underlying spirit and common law principles related to trade secret protection inferred from California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. By allowing indefinite retention and use for internal R&D, it increases the risk of misappropriation or reverse engineering of confidential information, which could unfairly restrain future competition or innovation based on the disclosing party's trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original contract's obligation to return or destroy confidential information. The Law Explanation states the change contradicts common law principles related to trade secret protection. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (BPC 16600) primarily addresses contracts restraining trade, the Law Explanation infers a contradiction to the 'underlying spirit' of protecting trade secrets. The change, allowing indefinite retention and use for internal R&D, increases the risk of misappropriation of confidential information, potentially impacting future competition based on the disclosing party's trade secrets, which the original text sought to prevent.
